CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and readable (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (interactive definitions, alignment-based chart extension, Voxelurn deployment). Notation and presentation are mostly consistent and examples/figures appear effective, and limitations of the approach are candidly described (conservative induction heuristics, restricted chart-extension conditions). From a formalist perspective, a few methodological components (the alignment heuristics and rule-induction criteria) would benefit from more precise formalization and clearer parameter reporting, which prevents a top score for presentation rigor. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (interactive definition framework, alignment-based chart extension) with a clear flow from method to experiments; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Notation and presentation appear consistent enough for comprehension, and examples (Voxelurn results, usage statistics) make the system behavior tangible. Minor presentation gaps remain — datasets and hyperparameters aren’t reported and some conservative design choices are spread across the method and limitations sections — which prevent a top score despite the paper being a clear, practical read for deployable systems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and readable (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (interactive definitions, alignment-based chart extension, Voxelurn deployment). Notation and presentation are mostly consistent and examples/figures appear effective, and limitations of the approach are candidly described (conservative induction heuristics, restricted chart-extension conditions). From a formalist perspective, a few methodological components (the alignment heuristics and rule-induction criteria) would benefit from more precise formalization and clearer parameter reporting, which prevents a top score for presentation rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (interactive definition framework, alignment-based chart extension) with a clear flow from method to experiments; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Notation and presentation appear consistent enough for comprehension, and examples (Voxelurn results, usage statistics) make the system behavior tangible. Minor presentation gaps remain — datasets and hyperparameters aren’t reported and some conservative design choices are spread across the method and limitations sections — which prevent a top score despite the paper being a clear, practical read for deployable systems.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper includes a comparison to an interactive supervision baseline (Wang et al., 2016) but otherwise lacks comparisons to other standard semantic-parsing or grammar-induction baselines and does not report held-out generalization metrics or reproducible experimental conditions. It is unclear whether the baseline runs were performed under identical data/metrics and no hyperparameters, datasets, or code release are reported, which prevents assessing fairness. The authors acknowledge some limitations of prior induced-rule approaches but do not analyze when those methods perform comparably or better, nor provide systematic ablations against competing techniques. Given these omissions the empirical comparison is present but incomplete and insufficiently controlled. | B(3): The paper compares to a relevant prior interactive-supervision method (Wang et al., 2016) and reports concrete user-facing metrics (structures built, percent naturalized usage), which aligns with my pragmatic priority for real interactions. However, the comparison set is narrow—only one baseline is reported—and it is unclear whether evaluations were run under identical conditions or with other modern semantic-parsing/grammar-induction alternatives. The manuscript acknowledges limitations of induced rules and discusses related work adequately, but missing additional baselines and clearer fairness details prevent a higher score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes a comparison to an interactive supervision baseline (Wang et al., 2016) but otherwise lacks comparisons to other standard semantic-parsing or grammar-induction baselines and does not report held-out generalization metrics or reproducible experimental conditions. It is unclear whether the baseline runs were performed under identical data/metrics and no hyperparameters, datasets, or code release are reported, which prevents assessing fairness. The authors acknowledge some limitations of prior induced-rule approaches but do not analyze when those methods perform comparably or better, nor provide systematic ablations against competing techniques. Given these omissions the empirical comparison is present but incomplete and insufficiently controlled.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to a relevant prior interactive-supervision method (Wang et al., 2016) and reports concrete user-facing metrics (structures built, percent naturalized usage), which aligns with my pragmatic priority for real interactions. However, the comparison set is narrow—only one baseline is reported—and it is unclear whether evaluations were run under identical conditions or with other modern semantic-parsing/grammar-induction alternatives. The manuscript acknowledges limitations of induced rules and discusses related work adequately, but missing additional baselines and clearer fairness details prevent a higher score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear, testable claims and provides a convincing deployed-system empirical demonstration, but lacks formal guarantees, algorithmic analysis, and full reproducibility details (hyperparameters, baselines, and statistical controls). These omissions weaken theoretical claims but do not invalidate the central experimental contribution, so a slightly positive score is warranted while noting the methodological gaps that prevent a higher rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides an applied experimental demonstration (Voxelurn, 240 structures, 74.7% naturalized utterances), but it lacks the formal guarantees and rigorous held-out generalization metrics my methodology requires. Mathematical/algorithmic aspects are presented as conservative heuristics (alignment constraints) without proofs of correctness or complexity analysis; evaluation omits standard quantitative generalization baselines, hyperparameter reporting, and statistical controls. These omissions create logical gaps in asserting broad generalization benefits, though the empirical signal is suggestive rather than invalidating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and supports them with a deployed system study (users taught the system, producing 240 complex structures and 74.7% naturalized-language usage), and it gives a plausible technical mechanism (conservative alignment-based rule induction and chart extension) with acknowledged limitations. Mathematical content appears limited to procedural heuristics; nothing in the summary indicates incorrect derivations, but formal analysis is also limited. My main technical concerns are missing details that affect reproducibility and robustness: no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistics on usage gains, and a single baseline comparison. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the central experimental claims, so I rate it a 4.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work builds on existing interactive semantic-parsing and grammar-induction ideas and is not a radical theoretical breakthrough. However, the combination of alignment-driven derivation transplantation with interactive definitions, together with a real-world deployment and tangible empirical gains, represents a practically useful and clearly articulated advance beyond minor engineering tweaks, so a moderately strong originality score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines interactive definition-based learning with heuristic, alignment-based chart extension to scale a core programming language — an approach that is a sensible and pragmatic synthesis rather than a radically new paradigm. The main technical novelty appears to be the alignment-driven transplantation of derivations (with conservative constraints) applied in the Voxelurn setting; however, this is an incremental extension of existing interactive semantic-parsing and grammar-induction ideas (e.g., candidate-selection interactive supervision) and relies on engineering heuristics rather than new formal formulations or guarantees. Given the reliance on conservative, ad-hoc constraints and the lack of theoretical novelty, the contribution is moderately novel but not surprising to an expert in the area.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper presents a notable and practically useful extension to interactive semantic parsing by combining user-supplied head→body definitions with an alignment-based chart-extension procedure to generalize paraphrases—an approach that is not merely incremental engineering but a clearly articulated method for bootstrapping a language. The idea of transplanting derivations via conservative alignment heuristics and applying it in a deployed Voxelurn community (240 complex structures; 74.7% naturalized usage) differentiates it from prior candidate-selection interactive supervision work (Wang et al., 2016). While elements (grammar induction, chart manipulation, interactive definitions) build on existing techniques, their particular combination and the practical deployment make the contribution a notable advance rather than a minor tweak, though it stops short of a radical new paradigm.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a meaningful, well-demonstrated practical contribution by enabling community-driven naturalization of a core language and showing strong real-world uptake (240 complex structures; 74.7% of recent utterances). However, they also concur the work is primarily engineering/heuristic-driven, with conservative generalization and limited theoretical guarantees or reproducibility details reported. Given the clear empirical impact but noted limitations in theory and reporting, a slightly above-midpoint score reflects its strong practical value while acknowledging the weaknesses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important practical problem—scaling semantic interfaces by letting users ‘‘naturalize’’ a core programming language—and demonstrates this at scale with real users producing 240 complex structures and a high adoption rate (74.7% of recent utterances). However, from my theory-oriented perspective the contribution is largely engineering and heuristic-driven (alignment-based chart extension with conservative constraints) with limited evidence of formal guarantees, no reported datasets or hyperparameters, and few systematic quantitative baselines beyond one interactive prior. These limitations reduce confidence that the technique will change foundational thinking, though the demonstrated community-driven scalability and the interactive-definition paradigm do open useful, pragmatic research directions that others may build on.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—making programming-language-like interfaces end-user extensible via interactive definitions—and demonstrates real-world impact: a user community built 240 complex voxel structures and the naturalized language accounted for 74.7% of the last 10K utterances. Its alignment-based chart-extension and conservative rule induction are pragmatic techniques that lower the barrier for non-experts to extend semantics and are likely to influence practitioners building interactive NL→program systems. The contribution stops short of being transformative because generalization is conservatively constrained, multi-utterance definitions are not fully handled, and key reproducibility details (code/hyperparameters) are not reported in the summary.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a highly relevant interactive semantic-parsing problem and provides compelling real-world, human-in-the-loop evaluation demonstrating practical impact. Reviewer A rightly flags shortcomings in rigorous held-out generalization, reproducibility details, and formal analysis, while Reviewer B highlights strong empirical community usage and appropriate scope. Given the solid contribution and practical results but noticeable methodological/reporting gaps, a score of 4 reflects its suitability for the venue pending stronger evaluation and reproducibility work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP/semantic-parsing topic (interactive induction of grammar rules and chart extension) and targets the right audience for top NLP/ML venues given its implementation in SEMPRE and a user-study in Voxelurn. However, from my perspective as a theorist the evaluation lacks rigorous held-out generalization metrics, hyperparameter reporting, and formal analyses of when their conservative alignment heuristics succeed or fail; empirical evidence is largely community-driven and task-specific (240 structures, 74.7% usage) rather than systematically benchmarked. The work is promising and positioned near the venue, but the absence of reproducibility details, controlled comparisons beyond a single prior interactive baseline, and formal guarantees makes it a borderline fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the top-tier NLP/ML scope: interactive semantic parsing and language grounding for programming-style action spaces are core venue topics. The work targets the right audience by combining model induction, grammar learning, and human-in-the-loop evaluation, and it evaluates with real community interaction metrics (240 complex artifacts built; 74.7% naturalized usage) that demonstrate practical impact. The problem formulation (core language + user definitions) and methods (conservative alignment-based rule induction, chart extension in SEMPRE) match standards expected at these conferences, and the paper is well positioned within the semantic parsing and interactive learning community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper shows promising real-world use (community-built artifacts and a strong naturalization result) and provides a useful comparison to an interactive baseline, but they also agree that empirical rigor is limited by missing hyperparameters, code/dataset release, held-out generalization metrics, and systematic ablations/sensitivity analyses for key heuristics. Given these clear practical strengths but substantial reproducibility and analysis gaps, a middle score reflects the contribution’s promise while noting it falls short of the empirical standards expected for stronger endorsement.

reviewer_a.justification: The empirical evidence is shallow: usage statistics and counts of built structures are reported, but there is no standard dataset, limited baselines (only one interactive baseline), no hyperparameter or code release, and no held-out generalization metrics as my field requires. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear absent despite many heuristic design choices (alignment thresholds, chart-extension limits) that critically affect performance. Related work coverage is adequate and the paper acknowledges some failure modes and conservative constraints, but reproducibility is weak (missing implementation/hyperparams) and there are no formal guarantees or systematic evaluations of when the heuristics break. Given these gaps, the paper lacks the rigorous, controlled empirical validation and analyses I expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper demonstrates real-world use (community built 240 structures, naturalized language reached 74.7%) and compares to an interactive supervision baseline, which supports the central claim that interactive definitions scale a core language. However, key experimental analyses are missing: no hyperparameters or replication details are reported, code/datasets aren’t mentioned, and there’s little evidence of systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses for the alignment/chart-extension heuristics. Related work is covered and limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility and deeper empirical probing remain gaps for a systems-focused contribution like this.

main_claims: A system that lets users naturalize a core programming language by interactively defining new utterances (head→body definitions) to scale language understanding for complex action spaces.
Using interactive definitions plus an alignment-based chart-extension procedure increases generalization from paraphrase-style definitions and enables reuse of new concepts in other definitions.

Applied to the Voxelurn voxel-building environment, a community of users taught the system and built 240 complex voxel structures, and usage of the naturalized language rose to 74.7% of the last 10K utterances.
_summary.methodology: Seed a core programming language and allow users to provide interactive definitions (head utterance + body of known utterances); induce grammar rules from these definitions and extend the parser chart via alignment heuristics (exclusion and projectivity) to transplant derivations and generalize paraphrases; implemented in SEMPRE.
baselines: Interactive supervision via candidate selection (Wang et al., 2016)
metrics: number of complex voxel structures built (240)
percentage usage of naturalized language (74.7% of last 10K utterances)
limitations: Low-precision induced rules significantly degrade user experience, so alignment-based rule induction is applied conservatively.
Chart-extension via alignment is only performed when the definition body consists of a single utterance (paraphrases); multi-utterance definitions are not extended.
Alignment-based rules are only induced when 1 or 2 tokens were left unmatched (conservative constraint).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic parsing / interactive natural language interfaces to programming

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in formal semantics and programming language theory with experience in semantic parsing and formal guarantees for learning systems.
traits: Penalizes papers that rely primarily on engineering heuristics without formal guarantees or analyses (will deduct points if no theorem/lemma about generalization or algorithmic properties is provided).

Requires held-out quantitative generalization metrics and systematic baselines (will penalize if evaluation lacks rigorous test splits, ablation over key components, or comparisons to standard semantic-parsing baselines).
Distrusts community-driven/interactive-only evidence unless reproducible and with controlled experiments (will downgrade if user data is anecdotal or lacks randomized/controlled evaluation).
Values clarity about failure modes and scalability (will deduct if there is no complexity analysis, limitations section, or discussion of when heuristics break).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, focusing on formal rigor, reproducibility, and whether the proposed methods have provable properties or systematic empirical validation.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Systems Champion
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with a track record of building interactive systems and deploying language interfaces, specializing in human-in-the-loop learning and user-driven language evolution.
traits: Rewards deployed or community-used systems and concrete user metrics (will boost score if real users produced substantial artifacts or high adoption/usage numbers).
Favors methods that enable end-users to extend systems without expert intervention (will reward contributions that lower the barrier to domain adaptation via interactive definitions).
Values practical generalization demonstrated via real interactions rather than only synthetic held-out tests (will be forgiving of heuristic components if they demonstrably work in the wild).
Prefers contributions that are accompanied by open-source implementations or clear instructions for replication (will upscore if code/data and instructions are provided).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Supportive of pragmatic, user-centered contributions that demonstrate real-world impact and scalability, emphasizing reproducibility and actionable system design.
